}

@media print {
  *,
  *:before,
  *:after {
    color: #000 !important;
    text-shadow: none !important;
    background: transparent !important;
    -webkit-box-shadow: none !important;
            box-shadow: none !important;
  }
  a,
  a:visited {
    text-decoration: underline;
  }
  a[href]:after {
    content: " (" attr(href) ")";
  }
  abbr[title]:after {
    content: " (" attr(title) ")";
  }
  a[href^="#"]:after,
  a[href^="javascript:"]:after {
    content: "";
  }
  pre,
  blockquote {
    border: 1px solid #999;

    page-break-inside: avoid;
  }
  thead {
    display: table-header-group;
  }
  tr,
  img {
    page-break-inside: avoid;
  }
  img {
    max-width: 100% !important;
  }
  p,
  h2,
  h3 {
    orphans: 3;
    widows: 3;
  }
  h2,
  h3 {
    page-break-after: avoid;
  }
  .navbar {
    display: none;
  }
  .btn > .caret,
  .dropup > .btn > .caret {
    border-top-color: #000 !important;
  }
  .label {
    border: 1px solid #000;
  }
  .table {
    border-collapse: collapse !important;
  }
  .table td,
  .table th {
    background-color: #fff !important;
  }
  .table-bordered th,
  .table-bordered td {
    border: 1px solid #ddd !important;
  }
}

.breadcrumb {
  padding: 8px 15px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  list-style: none;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-radius: 4px;
}

  display: inline-block;
}

  padding: 0 5px;
  color: #ccc;
  content: "/\A0";
}
.breadcrumb > .active {
  color: #777;
}
.pagination {
  display: inline-block;
  padding-left: 0;
  margin: 20px 0;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li {
  display: inline;
}
.pagination > li > a,
.pagination > li > span {
  position: relative;
  float: left;
  padding: 6px 12px;
  margin-left: -1px;
  line-height: 1.42857143;
  color: #337ab7;
  text-decoration: none;
  background-color: #fff;
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
}
.pagination > li:first-child > a,
.pagination > li:first-child > span {
  margin-left: 0;
  border-top-left-radius: 4px;
  border-bottom-left-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li:last-child > a,
.pagination > li:last-child > span {
  border-top-right-radius: 4px;
  border-bottom-right-radius: 4px;
}
.pagination > li > a:hover,
.pagination > li > span:hover,
.pagination > li > a:focus,
.pagination > li > span:focus {
  z-index: 2;
  color: #23527c;
  background-color: #eee;
  border-color: #ddd;
}
.pagination > .active > a,
.pagination > .active > span,
.pagination > .active > a:hover,
.pagination > .active > span:hover,
.pagination > .active > a:focus,
.pagination > .active > span:focus {
  z-index: 3;
  color: #fff;
  cursor: default;
  background-color: #337ab7;
  border-color: #337ab7;
}
.pagination > .disabled > span,
.pagination > .disabled > span:hover,
.pagination > .disabled > span:focus,
.pagination > .disabled > a,
.pagination > .disabled > a:hover,
.pagination > .disabled > a:focus {
  color: #777;
  cursor: not-allowed;
  background-color: #fff;
  border-color: #ddd;
}

  line-height: 1.3333333;
}

  border-bottom-left-radius: 6px;

@-webkit-keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
@-o-keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
@keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
.progress {
  height: 20px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  overflow: hidden;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, .1);
          box-shadow: inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, .1);
}
.progress-bar {
  float: left;
  width: 0;
  height: 100%;
  font-size: 12px;
  line-height: 20px;
  color: #fff;
  text-align: center;
  background-color: #337ab7;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 -1px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
          box-shadow: inset 0 -1px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
  -webkit-transition: width .6s ease;
       -o-transition: width .6s ease;
          transition: width .6s ease;
}
.progress-striped .progress-bar,
.progress-bar-striped {
  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  -webkit-background-size: 40px 40px;
          background-size: 40px 40px;
}
.progress.active .progress-bar,
.progress-bar.active {
  -webkit-animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
       -o-animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
          animation: progress-bar-stripes 2s linear infinite;
}
.progress-bar-success {
  background-color: #5cb85c;
}
.progress-striped .progress-bar-success {
  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
}
.progress-bar-info {

}

  background-image: -webkit-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
}
.progress-bar-warning {

  background-image:      -o-linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);

.embed-responsive {
  position: relative;
  display: block;
  height: 0;
  padding: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
}
.embed-responsive .embed-responsive-item,
.embed-responsive iframe,
.embed-responsive embed,
.embed-responsive object,
.embed-responsive video {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  width: 100%;
  height: 100%;
  border: 0;
}
.embed-responsive-16by9 {
  padding-bottom: 56.25%;
}
.embed-responsive-4by3 {
  padding-bottom: 75%;
}
.well {
  min-height: 20px;
  padding: 19px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border: 1px solid #e3e3e3;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);
          box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);
}

  border-color: #ddd;
  border-color: rgba(0, 0, 0, .15);
}
.well-lg {
  padding: 24px;
  border-radius: 6px;
}
.well-sm {
  padding: 9px;
  border-radius: 3px;
}
.close {
  float: right;
  font-size: 21px;
  font-weight: bold;
  line-height: 1;
  color: #000;
  text-shadow: 0 1px 0 #fff;
  filter: alpha(opacity=20);
  opacity: .2;

.close:hover,
.close:focus {
  color: #000;
  text-decoration: none;
  cursor: pointer;
  filter: alpha(opacity=50);
  opacity: .5;
}

Bug Reports</a></li><li role="presentation" class=""><a href="http://github.com/jzillmann/pdf-to-markdown" target="_blank" role="menuitem" tabindex="-1"> Code @Github</a></li><li role="separator" class="divider"></li><li role="presentation" class=""><a aria-describedby="popover-trigger-click-root-close" role="menuitem" tabindex="-1" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#"> About</a></li></ul></div><ul class="nav nav-tabs navbar-nav navbar-right"><li role="presentation" class=""><a role="button" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#">Debug View</a></li><li role="presentation" class="active"><a role="button" href="https://pdf2md.morethan.io/#">Result View</a></li></ul></div><div class="navbar-collapse collapse"><p class="navbar-text navbar-right">Spatial-Temporal Reinforcement Learning for Network Routing with Non-Markovian Traffic</p></div></div></nav><div class="container"><div><div><div role="toolbar" class="btn-toolbar"><div class="btn-group btn-group-md"><button type="button" class="btn btn-default">Edit</button><button type="button" class="active btn btn-default">Preview</button></div></div><hr><div><h2>Spatial-Temporal Reinforcement Learning for</h2>
<h2>Network Routing with Non-Markovian Traffic ⋆</h2>
<pre><code>Molly Wang^1 and Kin K. Leung^2
</code></pre>
<p>(^1) Department of Computing, Imperial College London, UK,<br>
jw923@ic.ac.uk<br>
(^2) Departments of Electrical &amp; Electronic Engineering, and Computing, Imperial<br>
College London,<br>
Exhibition Rd, South Kensington, London SW7 2AZ<br>
Abstract.Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been widely used for packet<br>
routing in communication networks, but traditional RL methods rely<br>
on the Markov assumption that the current state contains all neces-<br>
sary information for decision-making. In reality, internet traffic is non-<br>
Markovian, and past states do influence routing performance. More-<br>
over, common deep RL approaches use function approximators, such<br>
as neural networks, that do not model the spatial structure in network<br>
topologies. To address these shortcomings, we design a network environ-<br>
ment with non-Markovian traffic and introduce a spatial-temporal RL<br>
(STRL) framework for packet routing. Our approach outperforms tradi-<br>
tional baselines by more than 19% during training and 7% for inference<br>
despite a change in network topology.<br>
Keywords:reinforcement learning, packet routing, non-Markovian traf-<br>
fic, spatial-temporal modeling<br>
1 Introduction<br>
Rapid growth in network size and traffic volume, driven by social media, video<br>
streaming, and 5G networks, has created new challenges for routing optimization<br>
in communication networks. One significant issue is inter-domain traffic engi-<br>
neering or Wide-Area Network (WAN) optimization, which involves optimizing<br>
traffic routing across multiple domains. These problems are often NP-hard due<br>
to the combinatorial nature of routing paths, making analytical methods diffi-<br>
cult to apply [1]. Traditional machine learning approaches to traffic routing often<br>
require labeled data (e.g., next hop, path), which may not always be available<br>
for large-scale networks due to operational and privacy concerns [2].<br>
Reinforcement learning (RL) offers a model-free approach, eliminating the<br>
need for a mathematical model or details of the network environment. Instead,<br>
RL learns optimal routing policies through interactions with the environment in<br>
⋆Kin K. Leung was supported by the EPSRC grant EP/Y037243/1 and the Dstl SDS<br>
Continuation Project.</p>
<h1>arXiv:2507.22174v2 [cs.LG] 31 Jul 2025</h1>
<p>2 First Author et al.</p>
<p>the form of rewards. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) improves RL by using<br>
deep neural networks to approximate routing policies, particularly suitable for<br>
complex communication network environments where traditional tabular RL<br>
methods become computationally infeasible. Recent studies have successfully<br>
applied DRL techniques to address challenging network routing problems [3, 4].<br>
Despite their popularity, DRL methods rely on the Markovian assumption,<br>
which is often violated by the non-Markovian nature of real-world Internet traffic<br>
[6]. Therefore, we use a type of recurrent neural network to capture such temporal<br>
patterns. Moreover, DRLs often use fully connected feedforward neural networks<br>
to make decisions, which do not model the spatial relationships (e.g., neighboring<br>
connections) in the network topology. To address this, we use a form of Graph<br>
Neural Network to reflect the spatial relationships.<br>
Therefore, this paper proposes a Spatial-Temporal Reinforcement Learning<br>
(STRL) framework to capture both spatial and temporal relationships in com-<br>
munication networks using Graph Attention Networks (GATs) and Gated Re-<br>
current Units (GRUs). In particular, we apply the STRL approach to solving<br>
the network routing problem. The contributions of this paper are:</p>
<ul>
<li>Propose an improved STRL approach to capture non-stationary patterns in<br>
network traffic utilizing GRUs with an attention mechanism.</li>
<li>Capture spatial relationships using GATs.</li>
<li>Demonstrate the importance of jointly considering spatial-temporal dynam-<br>
ics for better routing decisions</li>
<li>Demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method against change of net-<br>
work topology, which is not included in the training data.</li>
</ul>
<p>2 Related Works</p>
<p>Periodic Markov Decision Processes (pMDPs) have been proposed to capture<br>
periodic patterns in internet traffic [7], but they remain inadequate for model-<br>
ing more complex non-stationary patterns. On the other hand, recurrent neural<br>
networks, such as GRUs, can capture such complex temporal patterns. Convo-<br>
lutional neural networks (CNNs) have been used to represent spatial structures,<br>
but are designed for grid-like Euclidean data (e.g., images), making them not<br>
suitable for networks with arbitrary topologies [16, 17]. More recently, Graph<br>
Neural Networks (GNNs), which are designed to model arbitrary network struc-<br>
tures, have been applied in combination with time series of link traffic loads to<br>
capture temporal dynamics [21]. However, these approaches address link-level<br>
congestion prediction rather than the challenge of routing optimization as con-<br>
sidered in this paper.</p>
<p>3 Preliminaries</p>
<p>3.1 Reinforcement Learning</p>
<p>Reinforcement learning (RL) is a machine learning approach in which an agent<br>
learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment. The key compo-</p>
<pre><code>Spatial-Temporal RL 3
</code></pre>
<p>nents of RL include the agent and the environment with states (s), actions (a),<br>
rewards (r), and policy (π). Formally, the environment is modeled as a Markov<br>
Decision Process (MDP), defined by the tuple⟨S,A,r,P⟩, whereSis the state<br>
space,Ais the action space,ris the reward function, andPdenotes the state<br>
transition probability. At each time step, the agent observes the current state<br>
s∈S, selects an actiona=π(s) according to its policyπ, and receives a corre-<br>
sponding rewardr. The environment then transitions to a new states′∈Swith<br>
probabilityPssa′=P(s′|s,a). The agent’s goal is to learn a policy that selects<br>
actions in each state to maximize the expected sum of rewards over time.</p>
<p>3.2 Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)</p>
<p>Reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms are broadly categorized into value-based<br>
methods, which learn a value function that estimates the expected cumulative re-<br>
ward for each state-action pair (e.g., Q-learning, DQN), and policy-based meth-<br>
ods, which directly learn a policy mapping states to action probabilities (e.g.,<br>
REINFORCE). To take advantage of both approaches, we use the Deep Deter-<br>
ministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm.<br>
DDPG offers an off-policy actor-critic framework, where “actor-critic” refers<br>
to the use of two neural networks: an actor that learns the policy (i.e., selects<br>
actions) and a critic that estimates the value function to evaluate the actor’s ac-<br>
tions. “off-policy” means that the algorithm learns the optimal policy by making<br>
use of the data generated from a different policy called the behavior policy, to<br>
improve sample efficiency. As a result, DDPG employs four neural networks: the<br>
behavior actor (μ) and critic (Q), as well as their target networks (ˆμandQˆ).<br>
The “deep” aspect refers to parameterizing both the actor and the critic with<br>
deep neural networks. The optimization process is as follows.<br>
We first optimize the behavior critic network, parameterized byθ, which<br>
outputs the action-value functionQθ(s,a), i.e., the expected cumulative reward<br>
starting from statesand taking actiona, and then following the current policy.<br>
The critic is trained by minimizing the mean squared error loss</p>
<pre><code>L(θ) =E
</code></pre>

</code></pre>

<p>4 Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)</p>
<p>Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) are a type of recurrent neural network designed<br>
to model temporal dependencies in time series data [13]. At each time stept,<br>
the hidden stateht∈Rdsummarizes information from previous steps and is<br>
updated using the current inputxtand the previous hidden stateht−^1 :</p>
<pre><code>ht=f(Wxt+Uht−^1 +b), (2)
</code></pre>
<p>where the weight matrixWmaps the input to the hidden state space,Ucaptures<br>
the influence of the previous hidden state,bis a bias term andfis a nonlinear<br>
activation function (e.g.,tanhorReLU).<br>
Therefore, The GRU takes as input a time series matrixX∈RT×F, where<br>
each rowxt∈RF represents the environment state at timetwithF features.<br>
Here,Tdenotes the window length.<br>
The GRU is chosen over conventional RNNs because it mitigates the vanish-<br>
ing gradient problem [14] using two gating mechanisms: the update gateztand<br>
the reset gatert, which regulate how much past information is retained:</p>
<pre><code>zt=σ
</code></pre>

</code></pre>

</code></pre>

<pre><code>̃ht= tanhWxt+rt⊙Uht−^1 , (4)

</code></pre>

</code></pre>
<p>4.1 Temporal Attention Mechanism</p>
<p>However, GRUs may struggle to retain information from the distant past time<br>
steps. To address this, a temporal-attention mechanism is introduced to allow<br>
the model to focus on relevant points in the sequence using attention weights.<br>
These weights are calculated through a query key mechanism where three vectors<br>
are used: query(Q), which encodes what the current time step is searching for;<br>
thekey(K), which represents the characteristics of each past time step; and the<br>
value(V), which contains the information from those time steps.<br>
Specifically, we first obtainQ,K, andV by projecting the hidden states<br>
Hthrough the learnable parameter matricesWQ∈Rd×dq,WK∈Rd×dk, and<br>
WV∈Rd×dv:</p>
<pre><code>Q=WQH, K=WKH, V=WVH, (7)
</code></pre>
<p>into different representational spaces for querying, matching relevance, and ag-<br>
gregating information. As a result,Q∈RT×dq,K∈RT×dk, andV ∈RT×dv.<br>
Then, we calculate attention weightsαijwhich measures the relevance of the<br>
j-th past time step to the current stepias follows,</p>
<pre><code>αij=
</code></pre>

Then, we obtain the context vectorhat at timetby aggregating informa-<br>

<pre><code>Ha= [ha 1 ,ha 2 ,...,haT]∈RT×dv, (9)

Finally, we combine each hidden state in the original sequenceHwith its cor-<br>

nism. It is calculated as</p>
<pre><code>HA=WA(H⊙Ha)∈RT×d
</code></pre>

<p>6 First Author et al.</p>
<p>5 Graph Attention Networks (GATs)</p>
<p>To introduce GATs, we begin by discussing graphs, a data structure for modeling<br>
network topology. Graphs offer a natural representation for non-Euclidean struc-<br>
tured data, such as communication networks. A graph is defined asG= (V,E),<br>
whereVrepresents nodes (e.g., routers) andErepresents edges (e.g., links). The<br>
connectivity of the graph is encoded in the adjacency matrixA, whereAij= 1<br>
if nodesiandjare directly connected, otherwiseAij= 0. Although traditional<br>
graph representations arestatic, meaning that the connectivity between nodes,<br>
encoded in the adjacency matrixA, is fixed, therefore once an edge (i,j)∈Eis<br>
defined (that is,Aij= 1), it is treated as equally important at all times, regard-<br>
less of their dynamic factors such as traffic congestion or link delays. To address<br>
this, Graph Attention Networks (GATs) introduce a mechanism that allows each<br>
node to dynamically weigh their neighbors through the use of attention weights,<br>
which is discussed below.<br>
First, we construct feature representations for all nodes in the network.<br>
For each nodei, we define a feature vectorxi∈RK, which may include in-<br>
formation such as a snapshot or even a time series of delay at the node, or<br>
the delay on its adjacent links. Collectively, we denote the feature matrix as<br>
x={x 1 ,x 2 ,...,xN} ∈RN×K, whereNis the total number of nodes andKis<br>
the dimensionality of the feature space. To capture the relationship or relative<br>
importance between a nodeiand its neighborj, the GATs calculate an attention<br>
coefficienteijfor each pair of nodes as follows:</p>
<pre><code>eij= LeakyReLU
</code></pre>
<h3></h3>
<pre><code>aT[Wxi∥Wxj]
</code></pre>
<h3></h3>
<h3>, (11)</h3>

learnable weight matrixW∈RK</p>
<p>′×K<br>
, and∥denotes vector concatenation, and<br>

<p>′<br>
is a learnable weight vector that scores the relevance of each neighbor<br>
pair. The LeakyReLU activation is used to encourage sparsity in the learned<br>
attention weights, where only neighbors with strongly influence will be assigned<br>
higher attention scores, while less relevant neighbors are still down-weighted, but<br>
not ignored entirely.<br>
Sinceeijare raw, unnormalized attention scores, we need to transform them<br>

<pre><code>αij=

After obtainingαij, we next aggregate the neighbors’ information to update<br>

</code></pre>
<p>whereσ(·) is a non-linear activation function, such as ReLU or ELU. This allows<br>
nodeito selectively integrate information from its neighbors based on their im-<br>
portance. We collect the updated features for each node in the following feature<br>
matrix:<br>
ˆx={ˆx 1 ,xˆ 2 ,...,ˆxN}∈RN×K. (14)</p>
<p>Next, we describe how the agent interacts with the MDP-modeled environment<br>
and makes adaptive routing decisions by taking advantage of the GRU, temporal<br>
attention, and GAT modules discussed above.</p>
<p>6 STRL Algorithm</p>
<p>6.1 MDP Framework</p>
<p>First, we model the routing optimization problem as a Markov Decision Process<br>
(MDP). The state represents all information available to the agent at each de-<br>
cision step. Specifically, at each time stept, we define the stateS(t) as the past<br>
Ttime steps of node delays and outgoing link delays for all nodes. Specifically,<br>
for each nodei, letDti= [di(t−T), di(t−T+ 1), ..., di(t)] denote its delay<br>
sequence, wheredi(t) is the delay of nodeiat timet. For each outgoing linkk<br>
of nodei, letLti,k= [li,k(t−T), ..., li,k(t)] denote the delay sequence, where<br>
li,k(t) is the delay of thekth outgoing link at timet. The state is thus:</p>
<pre><code>S(t) ={Dti, Lti, 1 , ..., Lti,K|i= 1,...,N}∈RN×(K+1)×(T+1), (15)
</code></pre>
<p>whereKis the maximum number of outgoing links among all nodes. For nodes<br>
with fewer thanKoutgoing links, the corresponding link delay sequences are<br>
padded with zeros.<br>
Next, we define the action spaceAas the set of decisions the agent makes<br>
based on the observed state. At each time stept, the agent outputs an action<br>
vectorAt= [a 1 ,...,aN], where eachaiis a score for nodeithat measures how<br>
efficient that node is. Using these scores, we run Dijkstra’s algorithm to find<br>
the topkpaths from the source to the destination with the highest cumulative<br>
scores. We then use the selected path to route next round simulation ofMtraffic.<br>
After the agent selects an action, the environment responds with a reward. We<br>
define the reward at each time step as the ratio of system throughput to average<br>
end-to-end delay. The average end-to-end delay is the mean delay of all packets<br>
successfully transmitted from source to destination during the last simulation<br>
round. System throughput is defined as the proportion of traffic injected into<br>
the network by its source that reaches its destination within the simulation.</p>
<p>6.2 ST Architecture</p>
<p>Now we discuss how the agent makes an action. First, the agent inputs the<br>
stateS(t) into GRU, going through the gating mechanism to discard old and<br>
retain new information via Equations (3), (4), (5), then output the hidden state<br>
H∈RT×din (6) that maintains only relevant memory.</p>
<p>8 First Author et al.</p>
<p>Next, to improve the model ability to capture long-range dependencies and<br>
mitigate the vanishing gradient problem, we apply a temporal attention mech-<br>
anism to the hidden statesHgenerated by the GRU. Specifically, we first con-<br>
struct the query, key, and value representations fromHas described in Equa-<br>
tion (7). For each time stepi, we compute the attention weights that quantify<br>
the relevance of every past time stepjtoi, following Equations (8)–(9). This<br>
results in a new representationHA∈RT×d</p>
<p>′<br>
, as defined in Equation (10), where<br>
each row aggregates information from all previous time steps according to their<br>
learned importance.<br>
Next, we reshapeHAinto a per-node feature matrix of dimensionN×K,<br>
whereK=TN×d, such that each node is associated with a feature vector of length<br>
K. Intuitively, this means we are taking all the memory the GRU has collected<br>
across time and distributing it evenly across all nodes, so each node receives its<br>
own feature vector that captures both recent history and the current state.<br>
Then, to model spatial relationships, we compute the attention coefficienteij<br>
between each nodeiand its neighborj∈Nias defined in Equation (11). After<br>
normalizing these coefficients according to Equation (12), we update feature of<br>
each node by aggregating features of its neighbors weighted by their spatial<br>
attention, as in Equation (13). The GAT layer thus produces updated node<br>
features ˆx∈RM×N, which can be thought of as a rich summary of what that<br>
node has learned from both its recent history and from its neighbors in the<br>
network.<br>
Finally, we generate the action vectorAtfrom ˆxby passing it through a<br>
Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) head. The MLP consists of a sequence of fully<br>
connected layers with Leaky ReLU activations and dropout for regularization,<br>
along with the layer normalization to improve gradient flow. Notably, the MLP<br>
is constructed to preserve the dimensionality of ˆx, so the output remains of size<br>
Nand is used as the action, where each value represents the efficiency score for<br>
each node. The purpose of the MLP is to allow the model to learn complex,<br>
non-linear mappings from each node’s feature representation to its action, which<br>
will be used to influence the environment. The overall architecture is illustrated<br>
in Fig 3.</p>
<p>6.3 RL Agent Training</p>
<p>Using the action vector generated by the agent, where each value represents the<br>
efficiency score of a node, we enumerate all possible paths from the source to the<br>
target node. For each path, we calculate the total efficiency score by summing<br>
the scores of all nodes along the path. Dijkstra’s algorithm is then applied to<br>
identify the topkpaths with the highest aggregate scores, which are deemed the<br>
most efficient for routing the traffic in the next time step, with each time step<br>
processingP incoming packets. After each time step, we store experiences in<br>
the form of (state, action, reward) tuples in a replay buffer, from which a batch<br>
ofMexperiences is sampled to compute the loss as defined in Eq. 1. Model<br>
parameters are subsequently updated using the Bellman equations and policy<br>
gradient methods detailed in Section 3.2.</p>
<pre><code>Spatial-Temporal RL 9
</code></pre>
<p>7 Application of STRL to Network Routing</p>
<p>7.1 Topology and Dataset</p>
<p>Our experiment is based on the AARNet (Australian Academic and Research<br>
Network) topology from the Internet Topology Zoo, consisting of 17 nodes and 27<br>
edges (see Fig. 1). Each node represents a Point of Presence (PoP), and each edge<br>
denotes a network link between PoPs. For traffic data, we use real-world Internet<br>
traces from MAWI (Measurement and Analysis on the WIDE Internet), specifi-<br>
cally the Samplepoint-F trace recorded on April 5, 2025, at 14:00 JST, spanning<br>
15 minutes. Each entry in the trace corresponds to a packet and we aggregate<br>
the data to obtain packet arrival rates (in terms of the number of packets per<br>
second) during this time period. To examine temporal patterns in the traffic,<br>
we conduct an autocorrelation analysis on the packet arrival rates for the last<br>
100 seconds (see Fig. 2). Autocorrelation measures how current values in a time<br>
series relate to their past values at different time lags. Here, the x-axis represents<br>
the time lag in seconds, and the y-axis shows the autocorrelation coefficient with<br>
the shaded area being the 95% confidence interval under the null hypothesis of<br>
zero correlation. We find that autocorrelation remains significantly positive for<br>
lags up to 40 seconds, meaning that arrival rates from the past 40 seconds are<br>
predictive of the current arrival rate. This confirms the non-Markovian pattern<br>
of packet arrivals, motivating us to include the past 40 seconds of traffic data<br>
as input to the RL agent. In the next section, we discuss how these temporal<br>
dependencies are represented in our simulation for the network environment.</p>
<pre><code>Fig. 1: AARNet
</code></pre>
<p>10 First Author et al.</p>
<p>Fig. 2: Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of Packet Arrival Rate over 15 minutes<br>
with Time Lag in Seconds</p>
<p>7.2 Network Enviornment</p>
<p>We first introduce the processing mechanism at the node and link levels. Let<br>
λ(t) be the number of packets per second, and we injectλ(t) into the AARNet.<br>
Each nodeireceives a continuous stream of traffic and processes it at a rateμi<br>
(i.e., the number of packets being processed per second). The processing time at<br>
each node is then determined asλ(t)/μi. If the node is busy, the incoming traffic<br>
will be stored in the node’s buffer. After processing, each node will forward the<br>
packets to an outgoing link. If the link is busy, the packets will be queued in<br>
the link buffer. Each link then obtains the transmission timeT(t) based on the<br>
bandwidth demanded for the incoming trafficR(t), calculated as the product of<br>
the average packet sizeLand the number of incoming packets per second, and<br>
its own capacityB:</p>
<pre><code>T(t) =
</code></pre>
<pre><code>R(t)
B
, R(t) =λ(t)·L.
</code></pre>
<p>Then, each link forwards packets to the connected node and repeats the same<br>
processing as described. Note that the time interval between successive traffic<br>
arrivals is set to one second to align with the non-stationary patterns in packet<br>
arrivals in seconds in Fig. 2.<br>
Next, we describe how the environment is initialized for agent interaction.<br>
We begin with a warm-up phase in which traffic is injected between all possible<br>
node pairs, and forwarding paths are selected at random. This warm-up continues<br>
until the network reaches a steady state, where all nodes and links are sufficiently<br>
utilized. Following the warm-up, we run 1,000 training episodes, each consisting<br>
of 100 time steps. At each step, the agent interacts with the environment by<br>
observing the current state, taking actions, and receiving rewards, as described<br>
in Section 6.3. The key parameter values for the agent, environment, and training<br>
process are summarized in Table 1.</p>
<pre><code>Spatial-Temporal RL 11
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 3: STRL Architecture for Solving NSFNet Topology
</code></pre>
<p>8 Performance Evaluation</p>
<p>8.1 Baseline Models</p>
<p>We compare our STRL agent with two baselines: Spatial RL (SRL) and Temporal<br>
RL (TRL). SRL shares the same architecture as STRL but excludes the GRU<br>
layers to disable it from capturing temporal patterns in the traffic. Similarly, TRL<br>
shares the same architecture as STRL but excludes the GAT layers, so the TRL<br>
architecture cannot see spatial relationships between nodes. This comparison<br>
aims to show how missing either temporal patterns or spatial relationships can<br>
impact routing performance. We also assess the robustness of these approaches<br>
to network topology changes.</p>
<p>8.2 Results</p>
<p>As we can see from Fig 4, STRL outperforms TRL and SRL by 19.20% and<br>
19.33%, respectively, in reward, which is defined earlier as the ratio of the sys-<br>
tem throughput to the average end-to-end delay. This suggests the importance<br>
of consideringbothspatial relationships in network topology and temporal de-<br>
pendencies in Internet traffic for making efficient routing decisions.<br>
Next, we introduce a topology change to the network and see how it affects<br>
their performance. Initially, the node Armidale is a leaf node connected only to<br>
Sydney2. We modify the topology by adding two new links to connect Armidale<br>
with Rockhampton and Townsville. This will change the spatial relationships<br>
around Armidale and also cause the temporal patterns to change at these nodes.<br>
After 500 time steps of inference, the resulting performance is shown in Fig. 5,<br>
where STRL outperforms SRL and TRL by 8.65% and 7.40%, respectively. SRL<br>
can adapt quite quickly to this topology change due to its ability to access spa-<br>
tial relationships through the GAT layers, whereas TRL experiences a significant<br>
drop in performance. STRL, which combines both spatial and temporal infor-<br>
mation, can adapt with better performance in presence of the topological change<br>
when compared with the baseline approaches, SRL and TRL.</p>
<p>12 First Author et al.</p>
<pre><code>Fig. 4: Comparison of average episode rewards across STRL, STL, and TRL
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 5: Comparison of average episode rewards under topology change
</code></pre>
<p>Next, we illustrate how STRL leverages non-Markovian traffic patterns in<br>
its decision-making by visualizing the GRU hidden statesH. Each hidden unit<br>
encodes different aspects of temporal information at each time step. Our archi-<br>
tecture employs 40 time steps with 95 hidden units per step; however, for clarity,<br>
we only display the first 50 units over the first 25 time steps in Fig. 6.</p>
<p>The visualization shows how individual units in the y-axis capture various<br>
temporal dependencies for each past time step in the x-axis. For instance, the<br>
18 thand 19thunits maintain consistently similar values across all time steps,<br>
suggesting that information from the entire 25-step window is uniformly impor-<br>
tant for their predictions at the next time step. Other hidden units may capture<br>
short-term dependencies. For example, the 7thhidden unit places greater impor-<br>
tance on the most recent one or two time steps, as indicated by higher values at<br>
the beginning of the time window compared to the later time steps.</p>
<pre><code>Spatial-Temporal RL 13
</code></pre>
<pre><code>Fig. 6: GRU Hidden State Heatmap
</code></pre>
<p>9 Conclusion</p>
<p>This paper has proposed a spatial-temporal reinforcement learning (STRL) ap-<br>
proach for packet routing with non-Markovian traffic in communication net-<br>
works. The proposed architecture outperforms in terms of training performance<br>
and robustness when compared with the baseline approaches capturing only spa-<br>
tial or temporal dynamics. Several improvements can be made to the proposed<br>
STRL approach. For example, although a self-attention mechanism is employed<br>
in the STRL, future work can investigate transformer-based models [24] to bet-<br>
ter capture long-range dependencies in sequential traffic data. Additionally, in<br>
large-scale networks, a distributed approach should be considered to address the<br>
scalability issues.</p>
